coredata.dc:title: Construction of a solid-state fluorescent switching with carbon dots and diarylethene
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xinyao
Liuye, Shiqi
Ma, Xinhuan
Cui, Shiqiang
Pu, Shouzhi
coredata.dc:description: A novel photo-controlled solid-state fluorescence switching based on carbon dots (CDs) and diarylethene was designed and constructed. It was characterized by TEM, XRD, XPS, and FT-IR. The polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) was used to disperse the CDs and prevent its fluorescence quenching due to aggregation. The fluorescence switching not only had excellent modulation efficiency, but also exhibited good photochromic properties. Upon irradiation with UV light, the fluorescence modulation efficiency was determined to be 96.3%. The mechanism of the reversible fluorescence modulation was attributed to the FRET between the CDs (energy donor) and the closed-ring diarylethene (energy acceptor). This work suggests the strategy of constructing solid-state fluorescence switching is feasible, and will provide new ways for the potential application of CDs and diarylethene.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Diarylethene
Fluorescence switching
FRET

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and modification of the CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of DT-CDs and particle size distribution (insert).
FT-IR spectra of the CDs, DT, and DT-CDs.
The XPS full spectrum of CDs (A) and DT-CDs (C); deconvoluted XPS spectra of C1s binding energy of CDs (B) and DT-CDs (D).
The change of UV–vis absorption spectra of the DT-CDs upon irradiation with UV/vis lights.
Changes in fluorescence spectra (λex = 365 nm) of the DT-CDs upon irradiation with UV/vis lights.
The UV–vis absorption spectrum of closed-ring DT (red) and the fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs (λex = 365 nm) in ethanol.
Time response of DT-CDs to UV (254 nm) and visible light (λ > 500 nm).

The time-resolved fluorescence spectra of DT-CDs (A) and the mixture of DT and CDs (B), when diarylethene was in open-ring isomers (black) and closed-ring isomers forms (red). (λ
ex = 365 nm; λ
em = 467 nm).
Changes in fluorescence intensity of the DT-CDs at 467 nm upon alternating irradiation with UV and visible lights.
Color (A) and fluorescence (B) changes of DT-CDs in PMMA films upon irradiation with UV/vis lights.
The modification process of carbon dots with diarylethene.
Schematic illustration of UV/vis lights controlled fluorescence modulation of the DT-CDs.

head.ce:title: Construction of a solid-state fluorescent switching with carbon dots and diarylethene

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi Key Laboratory of Organic Chemistry, Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, Nanchang, 330013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Key Laboratory of Organic Chemistry
Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . Jiangxi Key Laboratory of Organic Chemistry, Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, Nanchang 330013, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Ecology and Environment, Yuzhang Normal University, Nanchang, 330103, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . Department of Ecology and environment, Yuzhang Normal University, Nanchang 330103, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Jiangxi Key Laboratory of Organic Chemistry, Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, Nanchang, 330013, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Key Laboratory of Organic Chemistry
Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel photo-controlled solid-state fluorescence switching based on carbon dots (CDs) and diarylethene was designed and constructed. It was characterized by TEM, XRD, XPS, and FT-IR. The polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) was used to disperse the CDs and prevent its fluorescence quenching due to aggregation. The fluorescence switching not only had excellent modulation efficiency, but also exhibited good photochromic properties. Upon irradiation with UV light, the fluorescence modulation efficiency was determined to be 96.3%. The mechanism of the reversible fluorescence modulation was attributed to the FRET between the CDs (energy donor) and the closed-ring diarylethene (energy acceptor). This work suggests the strategy of constructing solid-state fluorescence switching is feasible, and will provide new ways for the potential application of CDs and diarylethene.

A solid-state fluorescence switching was designed and constructed with carbon dots (CDs) and diarylethene, and the fluorescence switch is dispersed into PMMA films to achieve the solid-state fluorescence switching of CDs.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are fluorescent nanoparticles that show excellent physicochemical and optical properties that similar to the well-known semiconductor quantum dots [1,2]. It has excellent properties such as high photo-stability, good biocompatibility, and tunable luminescence, so it has great application potential in sensors, photoelectric devices, and lighting equipment [3–7]. After more than a decade of development, the preparation and application of CDs have been studied widely [8]. With the continuous efforts of researchers around the world, various functionalized CDs were successfully prepared. For example, Qu and coworkers adjust the optical properties of CDs by dopant-N atom to obtain a fluorescence quantum yield of up to 36% [9]. Wang and his team prepared a carbon dots with good dispersion in an organic solvent but precipitation in a neutral aqueous solution by one-step hydrothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine in sulfuric acid solution, which could be used to detect trace H2O in organic solvent [10]. Sofin and his colleagues reported the fluorescence emission characteristics of carbon films formed by carbon nanoparticles aggregation, and the emission characteristics of carbon films compared to solvatochromic effects of carbon nanoparticles were investigated [11]. Despite these reports, the preparation of CDs with fluorescence modulation performance is still subject to many limitations. In particular, the fluorescence of CDs will accumulate and quench in the solid or powder state [12]. The main reasons for the self-quenching of CDs are the excessive resonance energy transfer or direct π−π interaction like organic molecules in solid or powder [13,14]. In recent years, CDs has attracted great interest as a photoactive material in the field of lighting and display [15,16]. However, the luminescence quenching of solid-state luminescence devices is strong in the solid-state aggregation process, and its performance is not satisfactory [17].

At present, many researchers have extensively studied on this problem to expand the use of CDs in solid-state luminescent devices and optical devices. Deng et al. found that the blue emission of the CDs is largely dependent on whether the environment contains polar groups or not [18]. Gao and his team have developed high-performance CDs with new structure by controlling the nuclear and polymer structure of carbonization, and possessed the self-quenching-resistant solid state fluorescence [19]. Rodrigues and his colleagues reported a red emissive CDs powder with fluorescence quantum yield up to 75% [20]. Although, solid-state luminescent CDs can be obtained by these methods, the complex method path, inhomogeneous dispersion, and poor stability are the disadvantages of preparing solid-state luminescence CDs. Therefore, it is very important to develop new dispersion matrices to achieve a more stable and higher quantum yield solid-state luminescence CDs. As far as we know, PMMA has been widely used in optoelectronic devices as a commonly used dispersion matrix [21], but there is still no report of the PMMA being used as a dispersion matrix to disperse the CDs. Therefore, investigating how PMMA affects the fluorescence performance of the dispersed CDs is crucial for creating solid-state fluorescent devices based on the CDs with high quantum yield and superior fluorescence modulation performance. Furthermore, because the CDs did not have the ability to self-regulate the fluorescence, its application in many fields is limited. Therefore, it is necessary to construct photo-controlled fluorescent switching with CDs.

Herein, the CDs and diarylethene were used to construct a photo-controlled fluorescent switching system through covalent connection, where the CDs acts as the fluorescence emission unit and the diarylethene acts as the switching unit. After dispersing the photo-controlled fluorescent switching system into PMMA, a solid-state film with photo-controlled fluorescent switching characters was formed. Its properties to the UV/Vis light response were systematically investigated. The construction process of the fluorescent switching system was displayed in Scheme 1 .

All the chemicals and reagents applied in the experiment were purchased from commercial channels and no ulteriorly depuration before use. 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra were obtained using tetramethylsilane (TMS) as the internal standard on a Bruker AV400 spectrometer. On a BrukerVertex-70 spectrometer, infrared spectra (IR) spectra were run on KBr pallets. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20, 200 kV) was used to get the morphology. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) patterns were obtained on an ESCALAB Xi+, using Al Kα radiation at 12.5 kV and 16 mA. The fluorescence quantum productivity was measured on an Absolute PL Quantum Yield Spectrometer QYC11347-11. Agilent 8454 UV/vis spectrometer, Edinburgh FLS1000, and Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer were used for the collection of absorption, fluorescence lifetime, and fluorescence spectra in sequence. The light sources used were the MVL-210 visible lamp and the MUA-165 UV lamp. XRD spectrum was acquired with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å, 40 kV, 30 mA) on a Shimadzu XRD-6000 diffraction instrument. If not specified, all the tests were performed at room temperature and the test concentration of the sample was kept in 1.0 mg L−1. The silt widths of the excitation and emission light were 2.5 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and modification of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were prepared according to the method previously reported in the literature [22]. The detailed process was showed as follows: Firstly, ammonia water (20.0 mL, 25% ammonia by mass) was mixed with 3.0 g of citric acid to form a transparent solution. The solution was heated in a domestic microwave oven for 6.0 min (650 W). Then, the reaction mixture was moved to a tube furnace and heated for 2.0 h at 200 °C after drying in an oven at 60 °C. The reacted powder was mixed with 60 mL anhydrous ethanol and centrifuged in 6 centrifuge tubes at 8000 r min−1 for 5.0 min. Finally, the precipitate was washed for 3 times with anhydrous ethanol and freeze-dried at −78 °C for 24 h, and a black powder was obtained.

The DT was synthesized based on the reported method previously [23]. The 1H and 13C NMR spectra of DT were showed in Figs. S1 and S2. The synthetic process of the DT-CDs was exhibited in Scheme 1. Firstly, 5.0 mL of ethanol was employed to dissolve 10 mg of CDs. After being activated for 30 min at pH = 5.0 in the dark with 17.3 mg NHS and 95.8 mg EDC, 5.0 mL of DT ethanol solution (15.5 mmol L−1) was added, and the mixture was stirred vigorously for 6.0 h. Finally, The DT modified CDs (DT-CDs) were dialyzed in ethanol for 48 h (molecular weight was truncated at 1000) and stored at 4–8 °C away from light.

The Fourier transform infrared spectra of CDs, DT, and DT-CDs were recorded and showed in Fig. 3 . In the FT-IR spectrum of CDs, the peak at 3433 cm−1 was ascribed to –COOH group, while the stretching vibration of –C═O was located at 1705 cm−1. In addition, the CDs with carboxyl groups on the surface were also proved by the absorption peak at 1385 cm−1, which was the vibration coupling peak by the bending vibration of ‒O‒H and the stretching vibration of ‒C‒O [24]. The stretching vibration of ‒NH‒NH2 group in the diarylethene molecular was represented by two absorption peaks at 3437 cm−1 and 3310 cm−1, respectively. The stretching vibration of –CO in the diarylethene molecular was located at 1637 cm−1. The stretching vibration of C–F exhibited a peak at 1267 cm−1 [25]. After the modification, the absorption bands of ‒NH‒NH2 were vanished and the absorption intensity of –COOH decreased. The stretching vibration absorption peak of –CO moved to 1710 cm−1, indicating the formation of an amido bond between the –COOH groups on CDs and the –NH2 groups on DT. The results demonstrated that the DT molecules were connected to the surface of CDs successfully.

The surface structures of CDs and DT-CDs were also examined by XPS. There were three representative peaks in the full scan XPS spectra of CDs, C 1s (285.2 eV), N 1s (400.4 eV), and O 1s (532.0 eV) (Fig. 4 A). The results showed that the surface of the synthesized CDs is mainly composed of C, N, and O three elements, which was consistent with previous reports [26]. In the high-resolution spectra, the C 1s bond has three peaks at 284.7 eV, 285.8 eV, and 288.2 eV, which corresponded to C–C/graphite, C–N/C–H, and CO/COOH, respectively (Fig. 4B). The N 1s spectra exhibited two peaks at 399.9 eV and 402.4 eV presenting amine N and C–N, respectively. The N 1s was decomposed into two peaks, corresponding to CO (531.7 eV) and C–O–H/OC–O (533.3 eV), respectively (Fig. S4) [27]. After the surface of CDs was modified with DT molecules, the full scan XPS spectra of DT-CDs exhibited four typical peaks, which represent C 1s (284.8 eV), N 1s (400.2 eV), O 1s (532.0 eV), and F 1s (688.0 eV), respectively (Fig. 4C). The results showed that the F element in the DT molecule was introduced to the surface of the DT-CDs (Fig. S5). The high-resolution spectra of C 1s was deconvoluted into four peaks, representing the C–C/graphite (284.8 eV), C–N/C–H (285.7 eV), CO/COOH (288.5 eV), and CF2 (290.3 eV), respectively (Fig. 4D). These results provided further evidence that the surface of CDs was successfully modified with DT molecules.

The absorption and fluorescence properties of DT-CDs in ethanol were examined and showed in Fig. S6. The absorption spectra of DT-CDs showed two peaks at 205 nm and 264 nm, attributing to the π→π* transition of the conjugated unit in the carbon core and DT molecules [28,29]. A shoulder absorption peak at 326 nm was observed, which belonged to the n→π* transition from the CDs surface and DT molecular cross-section [30]. The color of the DT-CDs solution was pale gray. The DT-CDs solution has an emission peak at 467 nm, when the excitation wavelength was fixed at 365 nm. A blue fluorescence was emitted from the solution of DT-CDs under 365 nm illuminations. When the wavelength of excitation changed from 285 nm to 445 nm, the emission peak of DT-CDs shifted from 456 nm to 539 nm. The result was ascribed to the fact that the solution of DT-CDs has excitation-wavelength dependence, which was attributed to the different particle sizes of DT-CDs as well as discrepant emission sites on their surfaces [24]. The behaviors of the prepared DT-CDs were in good agreement with the previous reports [26,31,32]. When the wavelength of the excitation was fixed on 365 nm, the emission of the DT-CDs reached to a maximum, and the emission peak was 467 nm. The absolute fluorescence quantum yields (AFQY) of CDs and DT-CDs in solution were determined to be 12.8% and 8.1%, respectively. The decrease of the AFQY from CDs to DT-CDs was attributed to the transfer of electrons from the highest occupied energy level of the donor to the lowest unoccupied energy level of the acceptor, and the ground state electrons were exchanged [33]. The AFQY of CDs and DT-CDs in PMMA were examined to be 13.2% and 4%, respectively. The results indicated that the fluorescence of CDs was unchanged after it formed a thin film, which was consistent with the results in previous report [34]. However, after the DT-CDs were made into thin films, the AFQY was reduced, which might be attributed to the tendency of 3D intermolecular interactions within the films extend more non-radiative channels [34].

The photochromic behavior of the DT-CDs initiated by UV/vis light was investigated in ethanol (1.0 × 10−3 g L−1). As shown in Fig. 5 , the shoulder peak at 326 nm reduced gradually under the 254 nm light irradiation. Two additional absorption peaks at 368 nm (ε = 49 L g−1 cm−1) and 556 nm (ε = 47 L g−1 cm−1) formed and enhanced, respectively. Simultaneously, the solution color of the DT-CDs gradually turned into purple from pale gray, indicating the closed-ring isomers (DT'-CDs) generated. At the photostationary state (PSS), an isosbestic point at 342 nm was observed, revealing a two-component reaction process taken place [35,36]. Reversely, the closed-ring isomers DT'-CDs could change to the open-ring isomers DT-CDs with the irradiation of visible light (λ > 500 nm), and the color of the solution could also return to pale gray completely.

The fluorescence emission changes of DT-CDs solution were recorded, when it was irradiated with UV/vis lights. With the irradiation of 254 nm light, the emission intensity of DT-CDs solution at 467 nm decreased gradually and the peak shifted to 462 nm (Fig. 6 ). Simultaneously, the fluorescence color of the solution changed from blue to dark. At the PSS, the AFQY of DT-CDs was determined to be 0.3%, and the fluorescence modulation efficiency was calculated to be 96.3%. Reversely, when the solution was irradiated with visible light (λ > 500 nm), the fluorescence emission intensity at 467 nm could be returned to original state. The reversible fluorescence modulation process showed that the fluorescence switching function of diarylethene functionalized CDs could be achieved by UV/vis lights to convert the DT between the open-ring state and the closed-ring state.

In order to find out the mechanism of the constructed fluorescence switching, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of closed-ring DT and the fluorescence spectrum of CDs were studied at length. As shown in Fig. 7 , the absorption band (428–650 nm) of closed-ring DT and the fluorescence emission band (393–621 nm) of CDs could overlap perfectly. According to the results and the principle of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [37], there was a FRET between the CDs and the closed-ring DT. Therefore, we proposed that the reduction of the fluorescence emission at 467 nm was mainly attributed to the FRET. The main reason for the fluorescence reduction of the system was the radiant energy of CDs (donor) transferred to the closed-ring DT (acceptor) [33].

The fluorescence switching mechanism of DT-CDs was proposed and described in Scheme 2 . In the beginning, the DT-CDs exhibited a strong fluorescence emission at 467 nm, upon excitation of 365 nm light. Upon irradiation of UV light, the open-ring state DT transformed into closed-ring isomer, and the FRET channel of the DT-CDs fluorescence switching system was opened. With the excitation of 365 nm light, the radiant energy of the CDs was transferred to the closed-ring isomer of DT [38], resulting the fluorescence emission at 467 nm quenched, and the color of the solution fluorescence became black from blue. Reversibly, the open-ring state DT could be recovered, and the FRET channel of the DT-CDs fluorescence switching system was closed with the irradiation of visible light [39], resulting the system to regain the strong emission at 467 nm and the blue fluorescence. The results revealed that the reversible fluorescence switching behavior of the DT linked CDs system was UV/vis lights controlled process based on the FRET [40].

The response time of DT-CDs solution to light was measured. When the solution was irradiated with 254 nm light, the fluorescence intensity of DT-CDs at 467 nm was gradually decreased. The lowest fluorescence intensity was achieved when the irradiation time was 150s. At this moment, the fluorescence was quenched by 97% (Fig. 8 ). These results demonstrated that the transition from the open-ring state (DT-CDs) to the closed-ring state (DT'-CDs) could be completed within 150 s. On the contrary, the fluorescence emission intensity could be recovered completely within 150 s under visible light irradiation. The results indicated that the light triggered transition of DT-CDs from closed-ring state to open-ring state could be accomplished in 150 s.

The fluorescence lifetimes of DT-CDs and DT'-CDs were detected and recorded systemically. As shown in Fig. 9 , the fluorescence lifetime of DT-CDs was determined to be 3.95 ± 0.06 ns, while the fluorescence lifetime of DT'-CDs was determined to be and 3.09 ± 0.12 ns (Fig. 9A). In other words, when the DT molecule was in the open-ring state, the fluorescence lifetime of the DT functionalized CDs was longer than that in the closed-ring state. Nevertheless, the fluorescence lifetime of the mixture of open-ring DT and CDs was determined to be 7.70 ± 0.11 ns, while the fluorescence lifetime of the mixture of closed-ring DT and CDs was determined to be 7.72 ± 0.17 ns (Fig. 9B). All of the results indicated that the reversible photo-controlled fluorescence regulation of the DT-CDs could be attributable to FRET from the CDs to the closed-ring isomer of DT’ [41].

The reversible photo-controlled property of the fluorescent switching DT-CDs was studied by alternating UV/vis lights irradiation. As shown in Fig. 10 , there was no significant reduction in the emission intensity of the reversible fluorescent switching after alternating UV/vis lights irradiation 3 times. The fluorescence emission intensity of the DT-CDs reduced 23% at the “ON” state, and remained unchanged in the “OFF” state after 3 cycles. The reason for the decreased fluorescence emission intensity of the DT-CDs may be that the DT produced by-products in the process of photoisomerization, which consumed the closed-ring DT’ molecules that could be used for the optical control of the switching properties [42]. The results indicated that the constructed reversible photo-controlled fluorescent switching could be the modulated completely by alternating UV/vis irradiation, and has satisfactory fatigue resistance.

In order to prove the system also has photo-controlled fluorescence switching properties in the solid state, we dispersed the DT-CDs into the PMMA films. As shown in Fig. 11 A, at the beginning, the film is pale yellow. It was changed in to purple with 254 nm light irradiation. Reversibly, the color of the film was returned to pale yellow with visible light irradiation. The results indicated that the DT-CDs in the PMMA film still have excellent photochromic property. Under the lamp of 365 nm light, the film emitted a blue fluorescence. With the irradiation of 254 nm, the emission quenched gradually, and the fluorescence color turned into black (Fig. 11B). Alternatively, the fluorescence color could be returned to the origin with visible light (λ > 500 nm) irradiation. According previous reports [43,44], most CDs were self-quenched in the solid state due to the π−π interaction aggregation and the radiant energy of CDs dissipated in the form of non-radiative transition such as aggregation collision and energy resonance transfer. In this study, the DT-CDs were dispersed into the solid matrix material PMMA to maintain a certain distance between two DT-CDs and inhibit the fluorescence quenching. These results demonstrated that this method has great potential in achieving DT-CDs solid-state fluorescence switching.

In summary, a novel photo-controlled fluorescence switching with DT and CDs was constructed successfully. The fluorescence switching exhibited excellent photochromic and fluorescence modulation properties upon alternating UV/vis lights irradiation. The mechanism of the reversible fluorescence modulation was FRET between CDs (energy donor) and closed-ring DT’ (energy acceptor). Furthermore, the photo-controlled fluorescence switching could be dispersed into the PMMA film to prevent the CDs self-quenching in the aggregate state, thus achieving the solid-state fluorescence switching of the CDs. The results will provide novel strategies for developing novel solid-state photo-controlled fluorescent switching, and expand the practical application of DT and CDs.

Xinyao Wang: proposed the concept of the study and conducted the main experiments, wrote the manuscript, All the authors revised the manuscript. Shiqi Liuye: assisted in the experiment and data collection, wrote the manuscript, All the authors revised the manuscript. Xinhuan Ma: assisted in the experiment and data collection, All the authors revised the manuscript. Shiqiang Cui: conducted data analysis, wrote the manuscript, All the authors revised the manuscript. Shouzhi Pu: provided financial support, All the authors revised the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22263005), and the Jiangxi Provincial Natural Science Foundation (20212BAB203011).
